sentences.text: I think there are 17 [INAUDIBLE].
But this is Budapest, basically.
It's far out to the east.
Reel number two, tape number two, with Leslie Aigner.
Yeah, in Csepel, we lived in a-- a duplex.
It's a-- it was basically a three-room duplex.
A small front room, very small, and a great room, which was the bedroom, and a very small kitchen.
We didn't have no-- we had running water, but no bathroom of-- such as shower.
So we used tubs inside to clean ourselves.
It was-- for five of us, it was pretty crowded.
Because all five of us lived-- slept in one room, in the biggest room in the house.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And so, this-- this is where I arrived in 1943, and this is where I started my trade school.
And-- and my father was inducted, and my sister had to go into the factory, only three of us were left.
And we had to move into the ghetto area, which was a similar situation.
It was a house with two apartments, but it was a garden in the house, and that's where those pictures were taken.
And we didn't even hardly packed, unpacked when we got there.
Because in the ghetto, we lived maybe less-- about a month.
Approximately a month.
And then we were taken to-- the Hungarian Nazi at Arrow Cross ordered us to pack.
And we were taken to Budakalasz, which was-- which had an abandoned big factory which they didn't use any more-- outdated.
And there were barbed wire around the factory.
And they collected from the outskirts of Budapest, not from Budapest, because Budapest had the ghetto.
And from the outskirts of Budapest, they gathered the small community Jews over there.
And they had 3,000, 4,000 together.
They loaded the cattle cars, and they were shipped out.
So where ours was, in Budakalasz, everybody slept wherever they could.
Out on the grounds, under the eaves of the brick dryers.
Or, ironically, even in the oven.
And so--
No.
I was in Csepel ghetto.
It was a small Jewish community.
We had a synagogue in Csepel, which had, probably, 50 members, or something like that.
I hardly got to know the Jewish community in Csepel, because I was there less than a year when we were-- when we were able to go to the synagogue.
Later, we were even restricted, especially when we were put into the ghetto.
We were restricted to go out after dark.
Bar mitzvah?
Yes.
That was in Nove Zamky, with my grandmother.
And my folks came down for the bar mitzvah.
This was in Nove Zamky in the synagogue where I grew up.
This was between my friends.
And this is why I say, in '43, I was moved into a new town.
I lost all my friends back there.
And I-- I just didn't have time, because of the turmoil at that time.
Didn't make new friends.
So when we were taken away from the ghetto, I was-- we were just huddling together, three of us.
Because most everybody was a stranger, even though we were all Jews and looked out for each other.
But we were among unknown people.
And this is how we were ordered to get up on the cattle cars-- 75, 80 of us.
And they gave us a half a barrel for necessities.
And they shut the door, and we were on our way.
We don't know where.
At night, half of the group were able to squat down, the other half was standing.
And then we changed.
The door opened maybe a couple of times on the way.
They threw in some food.
Ordered us to empty the barrels.
And we were on our way more.
Well--
It was a piece of board across, and people turned their back.
It was a crowded area.
And if somebody needed to use the toilet, it was just-- you turned your back to the toilet.
And that's how it was.
It was a necessity which you really couldn't live without.
Even though my little nine-year-old sister, at the time, she put her nose up, and she didn't want to.
And you can imagine, it was July.
The stink in that cattle car.
In the daytime, we were hanging on to the full window for fresh air and changing space over there.
I was mainly hanging together my mother and my little sister, to be together.
And so, some of us never made it-- the older people.
By the time we arrived, three days later, we arrived to-- as we learned, it was Auschwitz.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And they ordered us out.
I heard [GERMAN], and all kinds of swearing about Jews.
And the sticks were in their hand, and they ordered us in different groups.
And I was moved away from my mother to between a group of men.
Because children and women were separated.
Older people were separated.
And we were ordered to line up and leave the packages, the little belongings, that we might-- it might catch up with us later.
And in between the railroad track of Auschwitz, I saw my mother the last time.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My mother turned her head away.
My little sister waved to me a little bit.
And I marched front of Dr.
Mengele.
And I was motioned to the right direction, to the left, and went into the camp.
That was our goodbye.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] As we arrived in the camp, the sign, 'Arbeit macht frei,' even-- you know, I saw that and they explained it, what it means.
That you work, we'll free you.
Which we know what it meant-- nothing.
They shaved us.
They-- we had to undress.
And we were ushered into a shower, to take a shower.
And we got these Dutch clogs for our feet, and a cotton uniform, blue and gray striped, with a number on the uniform.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] That was our name from then on.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were ordered into the barracks.
And I don't recall-- so many people are talking about which barrack you were in, C, D, and I don't remember. 15-Year-old I was, and by now, I just don't-- can't even recall which barrack I was.
I know I was between two encampments.
There was two rows of barracks, and then a barbed wire, and then two rows of barracks, and the next encampment.
I was in a camp where a lot of Hungarians-- but a lot of gypsies-- were.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and the next in camp entered were the Russian prisoners of war.
On the other side was the twins encampment.
It's the-- where they collect-- Dr.
Mengele selected all the twins, out, and they did horrible experiments on them.
I saw them dwindling away, day by day.
Well, it was just they were losing weight.
Or just one of them.
And the other was helping the other twins.
And the-- they were all ages, from small children to adults.
But they were all twins in there.
I just talked to some of them through the fence, but it-- they-- doctors doing some terrible experiments on them.
But we couldn't really talk too much, because the guards on the tower, they didn't let us go close to the barbed wire, even.
You approached the barbed wire too close, they were free to shoot.
It was like open season on Jews.
They didn't have to account for it.
Several.
Several.
And I would--
Well, if somebody-- it happened that somebody met some relations across, and he run over to the-- to the-- or a friend-- he run over, not thinking, to the barbed wire.
About three foot from the barbed wire-- which at night, it was electrified.
But in the daytime, it wasn't.
And they run over to the street for distance, and started talking excitedly, recognizing somebody.
The guard gunned him down.
It's not once-- many times.

On the other side, in that Russian encampment, these Russian prisoners of war were ordered to work outside on the field, outside from Auschwitz, somewhere on the field, taking apart damaged airplanes for Nazi air force-- taking out usable parts.
At one time, five of them escaped, and their freedom didn't last too long.
They were brought back, two of them, and shot dead.
And the other three, right in the middle of the camp, they were hanged, and a place card was placed on their chest, 'I escaped.
I am here again.'
So this is one of the-- to go back a little bit, when I arrived to the camp-- this was in the following months-- and I described what was around me.
But to be in a camp, in the bunk beds, three high, eight, 10 of us on one level, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we shared blankets, one underneath us, one above us.
The dehumanizing condition that we went through, you didn't count.
You didn't-- didn't-- it was-- it was so that-- that you were ordered every morning, sun or rain or cold, snow, outside for a count.
They called it appel.
And so we had to line up and stand there and wait for the commandant to come around and take count.
So in the wintertime, we huddled together against the back of the barrack to keep warm.
And we were changing-- the last man went front, to-- because of the middle man was the warmest position.
And so we were changing for hours.
This was going on, sometimes from 6 o'clock in the morning till 9:00.
And when it came to feeding time-- I have to say feeding time.
It was such that the first man got a bucket in his hand, a-- a pan.
They measured in five, eight ladles of soup.
They counted down five or eight people.
And we sit around this pot, a spoon at a time, went around till it was gone.
Because I wasn't in Auschwitz more than a week, I realized food meant life.
You have to get your share.
But we watched each other, that everybody gets no more than their share.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Everybody was going to different for details.
I went front of Dr.
Mengele again, and again.
I wasn't selected into that transport.
So I stayed in Auschwitz.
And at one time, they asked people to work-- for work.
And I put up my hand, and I got a job on the kitchen.
I was very happy, under the circumstances.
Because I hoped that being close to the kitchen, I might be able to eat-- get some food.
But I was able to eat all the raw potato and raw cabbage I can muster down, because that's all we were cutting-- potato and cabbage, for months.
I was in Auschwitz four and a half months, which is-- very few people have been that long in Auschwitz.
Because I got this job in the kitchen, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was in a work detail, and I went into a barrack where people who maintained the camp.
In the meanwhile, people were shipped out to different parts of Germany to man the factories during the war.
Because the German people-- were-- were in the war.
And so they were no-- Jews were manning the factories and doing the production.
And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] transports were coming in, new transport, and going out transports.
Well, as I recall, there were machine gun guards on the outskirts of the railroad tracks, further away.
It's Jewish [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] prisoners who were ordering us around and pushing us around.
They were ordered to do-- separate the capable people, the children, and the women, and the oldsters, different groups.
And they were doing their job.
Not to me.
I don't know.
I just don't recall.
And if they were talking, they probably were talking in a different language.
They could've been Poles or Czech or Russian.
But different-- from different countries.
Because whoever arrived first, they get these jobs, which was good in Auschwitz.
They-- they were a job group.
They called it the Kanadians, which were taking apart the packages, and find all the food and the goodies.
And they were able to do a lot of things with their food and get favors with their findings, even between the Nazis.
No.
At the time, in July of 1944, I did not hear no orchestra.
I heard about the orchestra later, but I did not hear the orchestra.
At least, at that day, when I arrived, it was just the guards outside.
I saw some dogs-- holding some dogs.
But only the prisoners who orders us around already in a uniform, ordered us around into groups.
Leave your packages, and all kinds-- my-- my mother spoke German, understood German.
And so I just followed, or-- followed the group, whichever direction they pushed me.
Because I didn't speak any German at the time.
I learned quickly the necessary words, what I was supposed to know.
That when it's appel, you have to run outside.
And they ordered me around.
I-- anyway, I was working in the kitchen, at one location, and I was talking to the next fellow to me.
And I didn't hear the guard, the SS guard, yelling at me, shut up.
And he picked up a pitchfork, which we shoveled the-- the-- the cabbage into the containers to make sauerkraut, and threw it at me.
It went through my right foot, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was injured.
And a few days later, I wasn't able to stand on my foot.
And by then I knew-- it was already three and a half or so months I'd been in Auschwitz-- that a limping Jew is a dead Jew.
And this encampment where I was, there was a so-called hospital barrack.
It wasn't much, but at least I was able to get off of my-- they ordered me into the hospital barrack.
And as I was-- I-- you know, I was in the hospital, and they gave me some bandages.
But the doctor who was there was limited-- Dr.
Epstein.
He was from-- I think he was from Prague.
And he kind of took a liking of me.
And I was recovering a little bit, but I wasn't well.
A week or 10 days later, he came around and pointed-- you, you, you-- several of us.
Ordered us back to the barrack.
I was pleading with him-- Doctor, I'm not well yet.
If I go out, I won't make it.
He was so adamant about it.
He yelled at me, Raus!
You go back.
And I went back to the barrack, limping.
The same night, a truck stopped in front of the hospital.
And the hospital was emptied.
Whoever did not walk out prior night was taken to the gas chamber.
They did this every other month with the hospital.
I learned at that time that I was close to it.
Dr.
Epstein saved my life.
So here I was.
I didn't work any more in the kitchen, because they didn't need me by then.
I couldn't go in front of Dr.
Mengele.
Transport was still going in and out.
I mean, coming in new people and outgoing people who were ready to-- for work in the factories.
So I was desperate, because if I go in front of a selection, a limping Jew is a dead Jew, as I said.
I collect-- saved up a couple of portions of my bread.
And at one time-- this was early in December-- an outgoing transport was getting flannel uniform, the same color.
And we in Auschwitz had that cotton uniform.

So I saved up a couple of portions of my bread, and this young fellow who was already in the transport, he was walking among us because he was selected into the transport which goes out on the next day.
Walked up to him, and I offered him the bread in exchange for the clothes.
So I'd be in a transport without selection.
And he went for it.
And we exchanged clothes.
Afterwards, he told me, I wanted to change anyway, because my father is in the camp, here.
I wanted to stay with him.
And this is how I got into the transport.
We exchanged, except the tattoo.
I never got tattooed.
He was tattooed-- he was going out of Auschwitz, and I wasn't.
The only thing, I was having his number, and he got my number.
Luckily, they never checked the number on my arm, which I didn't have.
And this is how we were transported out from Auschwitz to Lunsberg, southern Germany.
Go ahead.
It's the nine-year-old, my youngest sister.
The older sister, as I said, was in a factory in Budapest-- outskirts of Budapest.
Marika.
Well, she turned her head away, because I think the people who were ordering them around, they told them that the men go separately into work camp.
I didn't understand German or the language, what they were speaking.
And so they separated me from her.
And she didn't reach out for me.
Or if she did, they talked her out of it, or something.
Yeah, I left this out, you're right.
When we were separated, as I said, I saw my mother and my little sister between the track-- the railroad tracks of Auschwitz.
When we went into the barrack in the next few days, it's-- it was just brutal.
And we got down to the animal level.
I mean, we were looking out for each other, but it was such a condition that you realized right away that life didn't mean nothing.
And I talked to one of the older fellows, 30, 35.
He says, those-- we saw the chimneys going day and night.
This must be the bakery.
And he laughed at me.
And he told me what it was.
And then I realized that whoever didn't come into the camp-- or at that time, I didn't know, and I hoped, to the last minute, till I came back, that maybe my mother is in the woman's camp.
But because my mother probably never let go of my little-- her little daughter, my little sister, probably, she didn't live another day.
This-- it was in the camp, in the first week, when I realized what's going on and what's happening.
And it was-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] it-- actually, something else happened before-- while I was in Auschwitz.
At one time, I was working in the kitchen, and we heard this gunshot-- first an explosion, and then a gunshot.
One of the crematorium was blown up.
I was there-- I don't know which month it was.
But I was there, and we heard.
And we heard it later on through the grapevines that-- that one of the crematoriums, the Sonderkommando, was-- got hold of some explosives and blew up one of the crematoriums.
They all were gone down.
None of them escaped.
But they--
The Sonderkommando was gone down.
None of them were able to escape.
But they destroyed one of crematoriums.
I heard the explosion and I heard the gunfire.
The crematorium was maybe five, six blocks away from my encampment.
I saw the chimney, day and night.
At night, flames were going out of the chimney, and the stench of the burned human body was always around us, constantly.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] And so, after that, as I say, I didn't work by then on the kitchen and of the period during the last month or three weeks while I was in Auschwitz.
And I was in the detail, emptying the barrels which were in each barracks.
They-- at night, we couldn't go out of the barrack.
So they had barrels for necessities.
And so we had to put this on the wagon and push it out on the field.
And after this crematorium was blown up, I witnessed-- ah-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Nazis made the prisoners dig big ditches.
It was like a foundation of a house.
Long ditches-- and they carried in a layer of wood, a layer of bodies, a layer of wood, a layer of bodies.
And they dose it with gasoline.
They were burning bodies in an open pit.
We were walking by this at one time.
[PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] And so as I say, after this, I exchanged myself for a piece of bread and got out of Auschwitz.
But this is my physical-- I-- things, what I mainly remember, the most horrifying, and-- I'm not talking about the daily beating and atrocity.
Well, the kapos, I don't know if-- later we learned that the different triangles meant different people.
And we had the gypsies with this color, and the Jews had  the yellow triangle, and the gays had the pink triangle.
And the criminals of-- the German criminals, because there were no prisons, they were the kapos-- the black triangle.
They were mainly-- they were the kapos.
There were some Jews, too.
But these kapos was-- were really brutal.
They wanted to make is sure that they were worthwhile for the job.
And they were inhuman and brutal with their stick.
OK.
